fact: The crew disabled the general alarm so it wouldn't wake anyone
source: National Commission on the BP Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill and Offshore Drilling, 'Deep Water: The Gulf Oil Disaster and the Future of Offshore Drilling' (2011), Chapter 4

fact: The Deepwater Horizon was drilling the deepest offshore well in history
source: The Macondo well (Deepwater Horizon) was among the deepest offshore wells at the time; widely documented. See National Commission Report, Chapter 2

fact: Pressure tests indicated the cement seal at the bottom of the well was failing, and engineers flagged the negative pressure test results but were overruled by managers
source: National Commission on the BP Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill and Offshore Drilling, 'Deep Water' (2011), Chapter 4; also BP's own internal investigation report (2010)

fact: Eleven men died in the Deepwater Horizon explosion
source: Widely documented. U.S. Coast Guard and Bureau of Ocean Energy Management records; National Commission Report (2011)

fact: Oil poured into the Gulf of Mexico for 87 days
source: The well blew out on April 20, 2010 and was capped on July 15, 2010 — 87 days. Widely documented; see U.S. Government Accountability Office and National Commission Report

fact: Multiple safety systems and safeguards existed but were overruled or bypassed in the lead-up to the blowout
source: National Commission on the BP Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill and Offshore Drilling, 'Deep Water: The Gulf Oil Disaster and the Future of Offshore Drilling' (2011), Chapters 4 and 5
